items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Pancreatic cancer is a significant cause of mortality, accounting for 3% of new cancer cases and ~8.3% of all cancer deaths in the United States. It has a low overall 5-year survival rate but shows improvement over time due to advances in treatment.
keyPoints: Third leading cause of cancer-related mortality in the U.S.
>66,000 Americans diagnosed annually
>51,000 deaths per year
Projected to be second leading cause of death by 2030
5-year survival rate: 13%
Lifetime risk of developing pancreatic cancer: ~1.7%
Incidence increasing about 1.1% per year overall; 2.36% in women <55, 0.62% in men <55
More common with age and slightly more common in men
5-year survival rate increased from 3% (1975) to 13% (2024)
44% for localized, 16% for regional, 3.2% for metastatic disease
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · Pancreatic Cancer | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Trends and Geographic Variations in Pancreatic Cancer Incidence
items.content: The incidence of pancreatic cancer is increasing, particularly among younger populations. It is more prevalent in developed countries and certain regions of Europe and North America.
keyPoints: Incidence increasing 1.1% per year overall
2.36% increase per year in women <55 years old
0.62% increase per year in men <55 years old
More common in developed countries, linked to smoking prevalence
Highest incidence in Eastern and Western Europe, North America
Tracks with smoking prevalence
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · Pancreatic Cancer | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Age and Demographics as Risk Factors for Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Pancreatic cancer risk increases with age, most commonly diagnosed in individuals aged 65–79. Males and black people have higher incidence rates.
keyPoints: Median age at diagnosis is 71 years.
Most frequent diagnosis ages: 65-79 (men 65-69, women 75-79).
Higher new cases and deaths per 100,000 persons for males and black people.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · RISK FACTORS

items.title: Impact of Smoking on Pancreatic Cancer Risk
items.content: Cigarette smoking is the greatest risk factor for pancreatic cancer. The risk persists for at least 10 years after cessation, with 25-30% of cases attributed to smoking.
keyPoints: Smoking correlates with increased risk and persists post-cessation.
25-30% of pancreatic cancers are caused by smoking.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · RISK FACTORS

items.title: Genetic Predisposition and Screening for Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Hereditary factors account for 10-16% of pancreatic cancers. Individuals with certain germline variants should consider early detection programs.
keyPoints: Hereditary factors: 10-16% of cases.
Increased risk with BRCA1/2 or PALB2 variants.
Family history increases risk significantly (4.6-fold for one first-degree relative, up to 32-fold for three or more).
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · RISK FACTORS

items.title: Understanding the Molecular Landscape of Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma
items.content: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is characterized by specific genetic mutations, with KRAS being the most common. Identifying these mutations can guide targeted therapies.
keyPoints: KRAS oncogene mutations are present in 90–95% of pancreatic adenocarcinomas.
Novel drugs targeting specific KRAS alleles exist and others are under development.
p16/CDKN2A mutation or epigenetic silencing occurs in >90% of invasive pancreatic adenocarcinomas.
TP53 is mutated in ~75%, and DPC4/SMAD4 in about half of these tumors.
BRCA2 gene mutations occur in 3.5–10% of cases.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · PATHOLOGY AND MOLECULAR CONSIDERATION

items.title: Precursor Lesions and Early Detection Strategies for Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: PanINs are considered precursor lesions to pancreatic cancer, with higher grades indicating greater risk. MRI and CT can detect cystic precursor lesions, aiding early diagnosis.
keyPoints: Most pancreatic cancers arise from PanINs, which have a progression model of 1–3.
High-grade PanIN lesions (grade 3) are not detectable by current imaging modalities.
Cystic epithelial precursor lesions can be detected on MRI and CT, potentially allowing early diagnosis.
Side branch duct IPMN is more likely to be noninvasive compared to main pancreatic duct IPMN.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · PATHOLOGY AND MOLECULAR CONSIDERATION

items.title: Characteristics of Mucinous Cystic Neoplasms in the Pancreas
items.content: Mucinous cystic neoplasms, more common in women and located in the body or tail of the pancreas, have a lower risk of malignancy but require monitoring for certain features.
keyPoints: More common in women and found in the body and tail of the pancreas.
Less likely to progress to malignancy compared to ductal adenocarcinoma.
Clinical concern arises with lesions >3 cm or those showing mural nodules/thickening.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · PATHOLOGY AND MOLECULAR CONSIDERATION

items.title: Recognizing Pancreatic Cancer Symptoms and Physical Signs
items.content: Pancreatic cancer often presents with painless jaundice, detectable when bilirubin levels exceed 2 mg/dL. Pain may also occur, described as mid-epigastric or radiating to the back due to nerve involvement. Other symptoms include light-colored stools and new-onset diabetes. Physical signs can include weight loss, a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign), migratory thrombophlebitis (Trousseau's syndrome), and Virchow's node.
keyPoints: Painless jaundice with bilirubin >2 mg/dL
Mid-epigastric pain radiating to the back
Light-colored stools due to bile absence
New-onset diabetes or hyperglycemia
Weight loss and muscle wasting
Courvoisier's sign: palpable gallbladder with jaundice
Trousseau's syndrome: migratory thrombophlebitis
Virchow's node: supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.title: Imaging in Pancreatic Cancer Diagnosis
items.content: Imaging is crucial following the clinical presentation of pancreatic cancer. Ultrasonography is used for jaundice, while CT or MRI are employed to assess tumor characteristics and staging. Dual-phase contrast-enhanced CT provides detailed visualization of tumor-blood vessel relationships, aiding in determining resectability.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography for jaundice
CT or MRI for tumor assessment
Dual-phase contrast-enhanced CT for arterial and portal venous phase enhancement
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.title: Histologic Diagnosis and Serum Markers in Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: A histologic diagnosis is essential, preferably obtained via a Tru-Cut biopsy needle. This facilitates molecular testing for genomic alterations like KRAS. Core needle biopsies can be performed endoscopically or percutaneously. Serum markers such as CA19-9, CEA, and CA125 are measured post-biliary obstruction resolution to guide therapeutic decisions.
keyPoints: Histologic diagnosis via Tru-Cut biopsy
Molecular testing for genomic alterations
Serum markers: CA19-9, CEA, CA125 after bilirubin normalization
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.subtopic: IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)
items.title: Management of Biliary Tract Obstruction in Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Biliary tract obstruction is common in pancreatic cancer, particularly with tumors in the head. Endoscopic placement of a metallic biliary wall stent can alleviate jaundice and pruritus. If surgery is considered first, stenting isn't always necessary unless neoadjuvant therapy is planned.
keyPoints: Biliary tract obstruction frequently occurs in pancreatic cancer with tumors in the head.
Metallic biliary wall stents are used to alleviate jaundice and pruritus.
Stent placement is not mandatory if surgery is the initial step but required for neoadjuvant therapy.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)

items.subtopic: IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)
items.title: Anticoagulation in Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer often experience a hypercoagulable state, leading to thrombophlebitis and deep vein thrombosis. Anticoagulation therapy is necessary unless contraindicated.
keyPoints: Metastatic pancreatic cancer can cause a hypercoagulable state.
Thrombophlebitis and deep vein thrombosis are common complications.
Anticoagulation with direct oral anticoagulants or low-molecular-weight heparin is required.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)

items.subtopic: IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)
items.title: Criteria for Resectable Pancreatic Cancer and Surgical Outcomes
items.content: Resectable pancreatic cancer is present in 10-15% of patients. Multidisciplinary evaluation is crucial, with upfront surgery or neoadjuvant therapy as options. R0 resection (no residual disease) leads to better outcomes.
keyPoints: Localized pancreatic cancer is resectable in 10–15% of cases.
Multidisciplinary evaluation is essential for therapeutic decision-making.
R0 resection results in 5-year survival rates of 50-90%, depending on tumor size and grade.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)

items.title: Management of Locally Advanced Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Locally advanced pancreatic cancer is characterized by tumor contact with arterial structures >180° and/or venous contact that does not permit resection/reconstruction. The median survival for these patients ranges from 6 to 18 months, with significant complications such as pain, biliary/duodenal obstruction, vascular thromboses, varices, bleeding, and infection/sepsis. Systemic chemotherapy with or without radiation therapy is the typical treatment approach. Approximately 20% of patients may become operable following neoadjuvant therapy.
keyPoints: Locally advanced disease defined by tumor contact >180° arterial structures or non-resectable venous contact
Median survival: 6–18 months
Complications include pain, biliary/duodenal obstruction, vascular thromboses, varices, bleeding, infection/sepsis
Treatment: Systemic chemotherapy with or without radiation therapy
~20% may become operable after neoadjuvant therapy
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED DISEASE (25–30% OF PATIENTS) | METASTATIC DISEASE (50–60% OF PATIENTS) | PATIENTS WITH A SPECIFIC MOLECULAR PROFILE IN THEIR TUMOR/GERMLINE

items.subtopic: PATIENTS WITH A SPECIFIC MOLECULAR PROFILE IN THEIR TUMOR/GERMLINE
items.title: Targeted Therapies for Molecularly Defined Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Patients with specific molecular profiles in their tumor or germline may benefit from targeted therapies. PARP inhibitors are effective in patients with BRCA1, BRCA2, or PALB2 variants due to defective DNA repair proteins. These tumors also show increased sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy regimens like gemcitabine plus cisplatin or FOLFIRINOX. Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) pancreatic cancers benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors such as anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4. KRAS G12C mutations, present in ~1% of cases, are targeted by specific drugs, while other common KRAS mutations are under clinical evaluation.
keyPoints: PARP inhibitors for BRCA1/2 or PALB2 variants
Increased sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine + cisplatin, FOLFIRINOX)
MSI-H tumors benefit from anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 inhibitors
KRAS G12C mutations (~1%) targeted by specific drugs
Common KRAS mutations under clinical evaluation
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED DISEASE (25–30% OF PATIENTS) | METASTATIC DISEASE (50–60% OF PATIENTS) | PATIENTS WITH A SPECIFIC MOLECULAR PROFILE IN THEIR TUMOR/GERMLINE

items.subtopic: MAINTENANCE THERAPY FOR PATIENTS RESPONDING TO TREATMENT | FUTURE DIRECTIONS
items.title: Use of Olaparib in BRCA-Mutated Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: For patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer harboring a germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation who have not shown disease progression on an initial platinum-based regimen, maintenance therapy with the PARP inhibitor olaparib is beneficial. It significantly improves progression-free survival compared to placebo.
keyPoints: Olaparib extends progression-free survival (7.4 vs 3.8 months; HR 0.53; 95% CI 0.35–0.82; p = .004)
Improves quality of life maintenance relative to placebo
Applicable for patients with germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · MAINTENANCE THERAPY FOR PATIENTS RESPONDING TO TREATMENT | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.title: Emerging Therapies Targeting KRAS in Pancreatic Cancer
items.content: Recent advancements have made targeting KRAS, previously considered nondruggable, a viable therapeutic strategy. Novel therapeutics are being developed to directly target KRAS and may soon become part of standard treatment for pancreatic cancer.
keyPoints: KRAS-targeting therapies show promise due to advances in organic chemistry and biosynthesis
Potential integration into standard therapy for pancreatic cancer is anticipated
Immunotherapy with personalized neoantigen vaccines or shared antigens like KRAS shows early promise
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · MAINTENANCE THERAPY FOR PATIENTS RESPONDING TO TREATMENT | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.reason: Most PanIN lesions will never progress to invasive malignancy and are not detectable by current imaging techniques.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; high-grade PanIN lesions are not detectable by currently available imaging modalities.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · PATHOLOGY AND MOLECULAR CONSIDERATION

items.assertion: A palpable lymph node in the supraclavicular fossa is referred to as Virchow's node.
items.reason: Virchow's node indicates late-stage disease and can be associated with peritoneal metastases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.assertion: Obtaining adequate tissue is crucial for molecular testing in pancreatic cancer.
items.reason: Molecular testing helps identify genomic alterations like KRAS, which can guide therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES | DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.subtopic: IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)
items.assertion: Neoadjuvant therapy is used to assess tumor biology and response to chemotherapy.
items.reason: This allows patients with progressive disease to avoid nontherapeutic, morbid surgery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why neoadjuvant therapy is used.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)

items.subtopic: IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)
items.assertion: Laparoscopy is always required before definitive surgery for pancreatic cancer.
items.reason: It helps in identifying metastatic disease which may alter the surgical approach.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because laparoscopy is selectively indicated, not always required.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · IMPORTANT IMMEDIATE CONSIDERATIONS IN PATIENT CARE | CLINICAL STAGING | RESECTABLE DISEASE (10–20%)

items.subtopic: LOCALLY ADVANCED DISEASE (25–30% OF PATIENTS) | METASTATIC DISEASE (50–60% OF PATIENTS) | PATIENTS WITH A SPECIFIC MOLECULAR PROFILE IN THEIR TUMOR/GERMLINE
items.assertion: Patients with pancreatic cancer harboring microsatellite instability may benefit from immune checkpoint blockade.
items.reason: These tumors typically have high numbers of mutations due to deficient mismatch repair.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of high mutation rates in these tumors explains their potential response to immune checkpoint inhibitors.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED DISEASE (25–30% OF PATIENTS) | METASTATIC DISEASE (50–60% OF PATIENTS) | PATIENTS WITH A SPECIFIC MOLECULAR PROFILE IN THEIR TUMOR/GERMLINE

items.subtopic: MAINTENANCE THERAPY FOR PATIENTS RESPONDING TO TREATMENT | FUTURE DIRECTIONS
items.assertion: Olaparib improves progression-free survival in patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer and a germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation.
items.reason: The hazard ratio for olaparib compared to placebo is 0.53, indicating improved outcomes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pancreatic Cancer · MAINTENANCE THERAPY FOR PATIENTS RESPONDING TO TREATMENT | FUTURE DIRECTIONS
